text: Based on the water discharge points and drainage range of the mining area, a segmented prevention and control approach is adopted. The water flow units are divided into the eastern, southern, and western sections of the mining area. Since the Yingjin River channel covers most of the southern and eastern sections and its seepage flow is significantly greater than that of the western section, the primary method used is a surface combined drainage approach focusing on "group wells for dewatering, supplemented by open drainage." At the same time, an underground drainage system is established with drainage tunnels in conjunction with dewatering wells, creating vertical and horizontal drainage and water diversion systems to control groundwater, thereby reducing the water flow into the mine. Specific measures include: in the western section, utilizing the natural slope of the Quaternary bedrock and the few discharge points by combining water diversion ditches with linear blind ditches for drainage; in the southern section, due to the numerous discharge points, a Φ300 mm sloped water diversion ditch is laid on the flat discharge surface, with linear blind ditches set on the southeast side, and dewatering wells placed on the outer side of the river channel, along with dewatering tunnels at the bottom of the wells to reduce river replenishment intensity and increase the curvature of the water flow path; in the eastern section, where seepage is significant and mostly located at the bottom of the river channel, a combination of water diversion ditches and blind ditches is used for drainage. Additionally, to ensure the drainage capacity of the internal spoil yard, a bottom mesh blind ditch is added for concentrated drainage measures.

Based on the actual site conditions, the fourth series base plate is located between 415 and 445 m, with the water level elevation above 431 m. The unit flow rate of the lower gravel layer in the aquifer ranges from 8 to 15 m³/h, indicating that aquifers exceeding 5 m can utilize drainage wells for dewatering. Considering the connection between the internal dumping site and the modified river channel, drainage wells will be used outside the southern bank of the modified river channel to control the groundwater level. If the water level rises, it can be replaced with a drainage well with a flow rate of 125 m³/h. The drainage wells are primarily used to remove water from the fourth series of pore water aquifers, and complete submerged wells are selected. The well structure consists of three parts: casing pipe, filter pipe, and settling pipe, with a well diameter of 500 mm, a pipe diameter of 350 mm, a wall thickness of 8 mm, and a total depth of 70 m. Since the water flow is mainly concentrated in the southeast direction of the mining area, the drainage wells are arranged on the outer side of the southern bank of the river channel to maximize interception of underground runoff, ensure effective drainage, and avoid adverse effects on the internal dumping site and river modification project.

The elevation of the Quaternary Southeast Gang interface ranges from 415 to 430 m, exhibiting an unconformable contact with the underlying Cretaceous coal-bearing strata. The drainage tunnel is arranged within the coal-bearing strata, parallel to the drainage well and located below it. The distance from the lower boundary of the Quaternary should be sufficient to prevent water infiltration. A combination of horizontal underground drainage wells and vertical surface drainage wells is employed for water discharge. The structure of the drainage tunnel includes components such as the main shaft, drainage tunnel, horizontal drainage holes, vertical drainage holes, underground water chamber, centralized pumping well, ventilation shaft, and water discharge valve room, forming a complete underground drainage system that effectively lowers the water level of the Quaternary aquifer and reduces water supply to the mine.

The methods for prevention and control include drainage by pumping wells, tunnel dewatering, support and water-stopping curtains, and combined dewatering methods. Pumping well drainage is a common technique that extracts groundwater to the surface for treatment or discharge, and the proper placement of pumping wells is key to controlling groundwater levels. Tunnel dewatering involves establishing a drainage system within underground tunnels to guide groundwater to collection wells or drainage wells. Support and water-stopping curtains utilize injected grout to form a sealed barrier, preventing groundwater from entering the mining area. Combined dewatering methods integrate various techniques to address complex geological conditions. These methods play a crucial role in mining and underground engineering, helping to ensure production safety and environmental protection. Many experts and scholars have also conducted research using these methods in different contexts. For example, Liu Kunpeng analyzed the issues of upper stagnant water formation and rising groundwater levels in open-pit mining due to regional precipitation and the softening of certain rock layers upon encountering water, determining that a combination of drainage wells and horizontal drainage holes should be used to achieve a reduction in groundwater levels.

The prediction of inflow volume in the mine is based on simulating the future inflow conditions of the No. 2 coal seam under different mining methods. According to field research in the mining area, there are several coal mines, primarily consisting of the Balasu Coal Mine, Yingpan Coal Mine, and Dahai Coal Mine, forming the Yuhong mining area. The Balasu mining face is 200 m long, 80 m wide, and has a mining height of 3 to 4 m, which has not been mined yet. The normal inflow volume for Balasu is 1324 m³/h, while the predicted normal inflow volume is 1475 m³/h. The coal-bearing area of Dahai is approximately 2700 km², and since Dahai Coal Mine is divided into northern and southern mining areas, the predicted inflow is 1786 m³/h. The predictions for Yingpan Coal Mine, Dahai, and Balasu Coal Mine are based on the planned mining progress. Simulations of the groundwater flow field after 10 years of multi-mine joint mining are shown in Figure 41. The predicted flow field diagrams for the Zhiluo aquifer at the end of the second year, the end of the third year, and the end of the fifth year indicate that as mining progresses, the range of the groundwater funnel gradually expands and eventually stabilizes. However, it significantly impacts the overall flow field of the region, particularly the damage to the Zhiluo aquifer and the No. 2 coal seam, leading to concentrated infiltration of groundwater into the coal seam and causing a continuous decline in water levels.

Analysis of the water inflow at the Yingpanhao coal mine reveals that a significant proportion of the inflow occurs at the working face. The main reason for this is the gradual connection of the water-conducting fracture zone generated during the recovery at the working face to the water-bearing sandstone layer of the 2-2 coal roof, causing the horizontal movement of water in the Zhiliu combined water layer to shift to vertical movement, which leads to an increase in the mine's water inflow and a change in the groundwater flow field. Data collected from observation points at the Yingpanhao coal mine was used to generalize the first mining area and input it into the model. Over two years, the total water inflow of the mine ranged between 169.0 and 534.9 m³/h, showing an overall upward trend. The normal water inflow at the Yingpanhao coal mine is 250.2 m³/h, with a maximum inflow of 534.9 m³/h. The increase in water inflow has created a significant hydraulic head difference in other water-bearing layers. This indicates that coal mining directly leads to the structural damage of the overlying water-bearing layers, causing groundwater to concentrate and discharge into the goaf, resulting in the drainage of the water-bearing layers.

The Yingpanhao Coal Mine began production in March 2017, initially employing a vertical shaft multi-level development method to primarily extract the 2-2 coal seam. During the extraction of the 2 coal seam, the Zhiluo combined aquifer directly contacts the coal seam and is a water-bearing layer that experiences water inflow. After water from the Zhiluo combined aquifer flows into the mine, the water level of this aquifer drops significantly, creating a substantial hydraulic head difference with the overlying Cretaceous aquifer, which also affects the changes in its water level. To better characterize the groundwater movement process under multi-mine joint extraction, this simulation is based on existing inflow data from the mine to model the groundwater in the Yingpanhao Coal Mine under mining conditions. Based on the extraction status of the first mining area, a non-steady, non-uniform, and anisotropic three-dimensional flow simulation of the Zhiluo group in the Yingpanhao first mining area was conducted. The simulation shows that the overall groundwater flow direction remains from north to south, with a slow hydraulic gradient. However, the mining activities have caused changes in the overlying water-bearing layers, and the discharge of mine dewatering has impacted the water cycle of the entire watershed, resulting in significant alterations to the water environment.

Based on the actual surface and groundwater circulation conditions in the study area, the advantages of the SWAT and GMS models are fully utilized. The minimum computational unit in the surface water model and the finite difference grid of the groundwater model are used as exchange units, coupling the two models under the influence of multi-mine joint mining. The coupled model calculations show that with the advancement of coal mining, the stable normal inflow rate at Balasu Coal Mine is 1475 m³/h, the stable inflow rate at Yingpan Coal Mine is 481 m³/h, and the stable inflow rate at Dahuize Coal Mine is 1786 m³/h. An analysis of the damage to the overlying rock layers due to mining reveals that mining has a strong destructive impact on the spatial structure of the groundwater environment, forming a groundwater drawdown funnel centered around the first mining area. This indicates that multi-mine joint mining significantly alters the regional groundwater system, leading to the dewatering of the aquifer and the formation of a stable groundwater funnel.

The Daqing Coal Mine, Yingpanhao Coal Mine, and Balasu Coal Mine are all located in the Hailiutu River basin, and their mining activities have a significant impact on the groundwater system. In particular, the Zhiluo Formation aquifer, as one of the main aquifers, is often directly exposed or connected through fractures during coal mining, resulting in a large influx of groundwater into the mines, which leads to a decline in the aquifer's water level and even localized dewatering. The dewatering from the mines not only alters the groundwater flow field but may also affect the surface water system, especially the base flow recharge of the Hailiutu River. Therefore, the phenomenon of groundwater dewatering driven by multiple mines working together is an important issue that cannot be overlooked in the management of water resources and the protection of the ecological environment in this basin.

Coal resources, as a fundamental energy source, have been widely mined and utilized for a long time to meet the needs of national economic development and construction. However, historical data indicates that the mining process inevitably has negative impacts on the groundwater environment within and around coal mining areas. On one hand, the extraction of underground coal seams cuts off groundwater recharge pathways and alters the structure of aquifers, which can affect the groundwater in overlying loose aquifers to varying degrees; water-conducting fissures connect loose aquifers, allowing groundwater to directly seep into mined-out areas, leading to the drainage of groundwater from the overlying fissured aquifers. On the other hand, coal mining generates a large amount of mine wastewater, causing sulfides to continuously enter the groundwater, resulting in the ongoing deterioration of the water quality in shallow aquifers. Additionally, the indiscriminate dumping of coal waste and other debris in mining areas is affected by atmospheric rainfall leaching, further contaminating the groundwater. As a pillar industry in Gaoping City, the coal industry has played a significant role in the city's development over the past few decades; however, this has been accompanied by a continuous deterioration of the regional groundwater environment.

In recent years, water resource issues have increasingly constrained the economic development of Gaoping, and the balance between water supply and demand has gradually been disrupted. In densely populated areas, the groundwater level has continuously declined due to the dual impacts of coal mine drainage and artificial extraction, with regional shallow groundwater being in a state of drainage or near-drainage. Currently, there are 34 large reservoirs within the research scope, distributed in a ring shape around the outskirts of Gaoping City; one area is severely over-extracted for groundwater, located near the urban area of Gaoping, covering an area of approximately 35 km². The type of extracted water source is deep karst groundwater, primarily used for urban domestic and industrial water supply. According to the statistical data from the Jinzhong City Water Resources Bulletin, the average annual groundwater extraction volume in Gaoping City from 2014 to 2019 was 4,844×10^4 m³, with the actual groundwater extraction volume exceeding the sustainable extraction limit for several consecutive years.

The Shanxi Group and the Shihezi Group fractured aquifer rock formations, as well as the thin-layer limestone karst fractured aquifer of the Taiyuan Group, are significantly influenced by tectonic activity. Under natural conditions, the primary recharge pathways are direct infiltration from atmospheric precipitation in exposed areas and overflow recharge from the overlying aquifer, which discharges to the surface in the form of springs in low-lying areas. In recent years, due to coal mining activities, the water level within this aquifer has been continuously declining, with some sections approaching a state of desiccation, and nearly all spring outlets in the region have ceased to flow. Influenced by the confining rock formations, horizontal flow predominates between layers, and there is poor hydraulic connectivity between the vertical aquifers. In the study area of the Ordovician karst fractured aquifer, the Ordovician karst water is generally deeply buried underground, with only a small portion directly exposed at the surface. Its recharge pathways, aside from direct infiltration in exposed areas, mainly include infiltration recharge from the Dan River channel, overflow recharge from shallow water through structural conduits, and lateral runoff recharge from upstream exposed areas.

For shallow aquifers, coal mining has caused the groundwater within these layers to be in a nearly drained state. Coupled with the direct impact of artificial extraction on shallow groundwater, this has led to a general decline in water levels in the shallow aquifers. On the eastern side of the study area, the terrain is predominantly exposed bedrock mountains, resulting in a relatively small effective infiltration recharge from atmospheric precipitation. Under the dual effects of coal mining and artificial extraction, the water level has generally decreased by about 2 to 8 meters, with the decline near the mining area increasing to around 4 to 8 meters. In contrast, the southwestern side of the study area features relatively gentle terrain with more extensive exposure of Quaternary deposits, allowing atmospheric rainfall to more effectively recharge the aquifer directly. Consequently, the water level in this region has generally only decreased by about 1 meter, with some areas even experiencing a rise of around 1 meter.

For deep aquifers, the drainage from coal mines affects the dynamic groundwater levels of deep aquifers through the downward overflow from the shallow aquifers. Additionally, for deep aquifers, artificial mining is also a primary controlling factor causing the decline in groundwater levels. Calculations indicate that between 2016 and 2020, groundwater levels generally decreased by about 2-6 meters. In the central part of the model area, the presence of geological structures such as sloped fault zones has created fracture channels that facilitate water flow in the aquifers, intensifying the hydraulic connection between shallow and deep aquifers. As a result, the downward overflow of groundwater increases, leading to a relatively lower degree of groundwater level decline in this area.

Analysis indicates that the primary factors contributing to the decline in groundwater levels in the region's aquifers are the drainage and discharge from coal mines, along with the artificial extraction of regional groundwater. These two factors jointly control the dynamic changes in regional groundwater levels. Therefore, based on the above conclusions, it is possible to make targeted and reasonable predictions regarding the dynamic changes in the groundwater aquifers of the study area, and to conduct research on the management of excessive groundwater extraction and the prediction of dynamic changes in regional groundwater under the conditions of coal mine drainage, providing reference suggestions for regional groundwater management.

In the study area, the water hazard type in the mine pit is primarily caused by the gravelly fine sand aquifer, which serves as the main direct source of water influx. The thickness of this aquifer ranges from 3.63 to 100.87 meters, with the groundwater level typically buried at a depth of 2 to 10 meters, and the water level elevation between 930 and 970 meters, classifying it as phreatic water with moderate water richness. Currently, the mining company mainly employs dewatering wells to reduce the impact of this type of water hazard, achieving good results. In response to the characteristics of this water hazard, measures combining dewatering wells and curtain grouting for water blockage have been designed, yielding favorable prevention and control effects. The dewatering wells lower the groundwater level around the mine pit, preventing groundwater from flowing into the mining area and ensuring the safe extraction of the open-pit mine. Water from this layer often infiltrates the mine pit through the boundary in the form of seepage, affecting the safety of mining operations; therefore, dewatering is currently the primary prevention and control method.

Due to open-pit mining, the structure of the aquifer has changed. Additionally, the dewatering wells at the pit boundaries have drawn down groundwater, resulting in a localized decrease in groundwater levels at the pit edges, causing some groundwater to flow into the pit and create water accumulation. Therefore, the drainage from the dewatering wells has become another important method for discharging groundwater. Under natural conditions, groundwater primarily discharges to lakes or areas outside through evaporation and runoff, but mining activities have altered the natural flow field, forming a groundwater depression funnel centered around the pit. The arrangement of dewatering wells not only controls the runoff of groundwater into the pit but also affects the recharge and discharge relationship between lakes and groundwater, thereby posing challenges for the protection of water resources in the lake area.

The purpose of this pumping test is to obtain the hydrogeological parameters of the aquifer, providing a basis for the design and numerical simulation of drainage wells. Through three groups of eight drawdown pumping tests, lasting a total of 6660 minutes, key parameters such as permeability coefficient, specific yield, and influence radius were obtained. The results indicate that the permeability coefficient of the aquifer ranges from 0.42 to 3.45 m/d, specific yield ranges from 0.117 to 0.191, and unit discharge ranges from 0.15 to 0.41 L/(s·m), indicating moderate water richness. These parameters are directly used for the calculation and optimization of drainage well discharge, forming the foundation for the design of drainage projects.

In the model, the drainage wells serve as the main discharge items. During the simulation period, a total of 251 drainage wells were distributed across different pipelines, with individual well daily drainage volumes ranging from 0.515 m³/d to 77.794 m³/d. The operational time and dynamic changes in pumping volume of the drainage wells were incorporated into the model to accurately reflect the groundwater drainage effects during the extraction process. The arrangement of the drainage wells not only affects the local flow field but also alters the hydraulic connection between the lake area and the mining pit, making it a core engineering measure in water control strategies.

The simulation results indicate that the actual pumping volume of the drainage wells in each scenario gradually decreases and stabilizes after one year of operation. This suggests that the drainage wells can quickly lower the water level of the nearby aquifer in the initial phase, while primarily maintaining flow field stability in the later phase. Scenario 1 (pure drainage) has the highest pumping volume, reaching 2.46×10⁷ m³, while Scenario 5 (drainage + long curtain) has the lowest pumping volume at 1.14×10⁷ m³. This indicates that curtain grouting can significantly reduce the pumping volume of the drainage wells, lower operational costs, and effectively control the decline of lake water levels, thus protecting water resources. The study shows that drainage from the wells is an effective means of controlling water hazards in mining pits, significantly lowering the water level around the pit and preventing sudden water inflow incidents. Combining curtain grouting can further reduce the pumping volume of the drainage wells and minimize interference with the water resources in the lake area. This research optimizes the layout and pumping strategy of the drainage wells through numerical simulation, providing a scientific basis for hydrological control in similar lake-adjacent mining areas.
